coredata.dc:title: Antibacterial nano-fibers of carbon quantum dot/titanium dioxide by electrospinning for the removal of particulate matter and volatile organic compounds
coredata.dc:creator: Kuo, Yu-Lin
Chen, Wei-Ting
Chen, Chien-Hua
Wu, Hung-Ta
Chiou, Chyow-San
Chen, Ying-Cih
Chen, Hua-Wei
coredata.dc:description: The rapid development of industrial activities has led to the release of particulate matter and volatile organic compounds, the two major anthropogenic pollutants in the atmosphere. To degrade those air pollutants, photocatalysts are commonly used; a high photocatalytic activity is beneficial for enhanced degradation of air pollutants. In this study, electrospinning technology was used to combine the QD/TiO2 with both polyimide (PI) and polyethersulfone (PES) to form a PI/PES nano-fiber membrane. The Taguchi method was applied to calculate four operational parameters (PI/PES content, QD/TiO2 content, operational voltage, and electrospinning time) with three levels for nine combinations of experimental conditions. The optimal electrospinning conditions of the QD/TiO2@PI/PES module comprised 40 wt% PI/PES and 5 wt% QD/TiO2 in the electrospinning solution, and the operational voltage of 20 kV and electrospinning time of 2 h was applied to obtain filtration efficiency and quality of 99.60 % and 0.151. The degradation rates of methylene blue and acetone were 45.5% and 87% with photocatalysis reaction via QD/TiO2@PI/PES. In the antibacterial test, QD/TiO2 was highly efficient in inactivating Staphylococcus aureus (100%) under ultraviolet (UV) irradiation of 4 h. Therefore, QD/TiO2@PI/PES membrane showed great potential in medical and industrial applications for the elimination of both particulate matter and volatile organic compounds.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalyst
Electrospinning technology
Taguchi method
Filtration efficiency

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photocatalyst tests using methylene blue and acetone
Analysis of filtration quality of nano-fiber membrane via Taguchi method (The larger the better)
Variation in filtration quality at different electrospinning time
Variation of filtration quality at different ratios of PI/PES
Variation in filtration quality at different operational voltages
Variation of filtration quality at different contents of QD/TiO2

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Science and Technology Council, Republic of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported in part by grant NSTC 112-2221-E-197-009 from the National Science and Technology Council, Republic of China.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of annular photoreactor: (1) Flowmeter controller; (2) Flowmeter; (3) Gas mixer; (4) Three-way valve; (5) Annular photoreactor; (6) Thermostatic water bath; (7) two-way valve; (8) GC/FID. 1B The device picture of filter testing system.
Morphology of synthesized TiO2 (A) and QD/TiO2 (B) by SEM (The proportional scale is 500 nm) and QD/TiO2 (C) and QD (D) via TEM; The QD solution was emitted without (E) and with (F) UV light.
The functional group analysis of TiO2 and 5.0% QD/TiO2 (A); PI/PES and 5.0% QD/TiO2@PI/PES (B).

Photodecomposition of 50.0 ppm methylene blue at different conditions and photodecomposition of 150.0 ppm acetone for commercial TiO2 (P25) and 5.0% QD/TiO2@PI/PES (the optimal membrane at Taguchi 5 condition). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Emission spectra of TiO2(P25) and QD/TiO2 in the range from 250 to 500 nm.
The variation of Staphylococcus aureus under UV light (365 nm) at various emission time (A) 0 h (B) 1 h (C) 2 h (D) 3 h (E) 4 h.

Variation of filtration quality at different ratios of PI/PES (A)10:90 (B)20:80 (C)30:70 (D)40:60; different operational voltages (E)17.5V (F)20.0V (G)22.5 kV (H)25.0 kV; different contents of QD/TiO2 (I)1 wt% (J)3 wt% (K)5 wt% (L)7 wt %.
Variation of filtration quality at different ratios of PI/PES (A); different operational voltages (B); different contents of QD/TiO2 (C).
Variation of penetration efficiency at different ratios of PI/PES (A); different operational voltages (B); different contents of QD/TiO2 (C).
Experimental results and S/N values of nine sample combination at different operational conditions.
Factor influence order analysis of four experimental factors.
Filtration efficiency and quality with pressure loss and penetration at different operational conditions.
Filtration efficiency and quality at different PI/PES ratios.
Filtration efficiency and quality at different voltages.
Comparison of filtration efficiency and quality at different filter materials.

head.ce:title: Antibacterial nano-fibers of carbon quantum dot/titanium dioxide by electrospinning for the removal of particulate matter and volatile organic compounds

Present/permanent address:No.1, Sec. 1, Shennong Rd., Yilan City, Yilan County 26047, Taiwan (R.O.C.).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The rapid development of industrial activities has led to the release of particulate matter and volatile organic compounds, the two major anthropogenic pollutants in the atmosphere. To degrade those air pollutants, photocatalysts are commonly used; a high photocatalytic activity is beneficial for enhanced degradation of air pollutants. In this study, electrospinning technology was used to combine the QD/TiO2 with both polyimide (PI) and polyethersulfone (PES) to form a PI/PES nano-fiber membrane. The Taguchi method was applied to calculate four operational parameters (PI/PES content, QD/TiO2 content, operational voltage, and electrospinning time) with three levels for nine combinations of experimental conditions. The optimal electrospinning conditions of the QD/TiO2@PI/PES module comprised 40 wt% PI/PES and 5 wt% QD/TiO2 in the electrospinning solution, and the operational voltage of 20 kV and electrospinning time of 2 h was applied to obtain filtration efficiency and quality of 99.60 % and 0.151. The degradation rates of methylene blue and acetone were 45.5% and 87% with photocatalysis reaction via QD/TiO2@PI/PES. In the antibacterial test, QD/TiO2 was highly efficient in inactivating Staphylococcus aureus (100%) under ultraviolet (UV) irradiation of 4 h. Therefore, QD/TiO2@PI/PES membrane showed great potential in medical and industrial applications for the elimination of both particulate matter and volatile organic compounds.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: The issue of air pollution has become a great concern recently due to dramatic climate changes and serious respiratory system disease [1–3]. The coronavirus disease (COVID-19) has spread to global countries for more than 2 years and has affected the air quality because viruses and bacteria can spread to the outside environment in the air medium [4–6]. Particulate matter (PM) and volatile organic compounds (VOCs) are two major air pollutants, and most sources of two air pollution come from anthropogenic activities, which dramatically influence air quality.

The size of PM changes variably because of the combination of different compounds from a variety of industrial operations and transportation activities. The most known PM substance includes PM10 (diameter <10 μm) and PM2.5 (diameter <2.5 μm), which are available to pass through the respiratory system into the inside of lungs, but the nano-PM (diameter <0.1 μm) can pass through pulmonary alveolus into the inside of blood [7–9]. In other words, the smaller size of air particle depicts a larger penetration ability, and it is difficult to expel them from the body when they have already entered. Moreover, approximately 99% of air particles are nano-PM, and most nano-PM cannot be filtered by mask even if people wear masks to block the PMs [10].

VOCs comprise carbon oxide (CO), carbon dioxide (CO2), carbonic acid (H2CO3), metal carbonate, and any carbon compounds involved with photochemical reaction (ex: benzene, toluene, xylene, and formaldehyde). The source of VOCs are mainly indoor decoration, industrial operation, semiconductor process, and optoelectronics manufacturing. They are of diverse categories, which may induce secondary reactions to generate many complex substances that are the main factors to dramatically degenerate air quality [11–13]. Based on this background, there are some treatments for PM and VOSs, including filtration, incineration, biological treatment, adsorption, and photocatalysis; filtration and photocatalysis render high efficiency, rigid stabilization, and low cost on the elimination of air pollution [14,15]. The use of fiber material for filtration has the advantages of large specific surface area, high porosity, adjustable fiber diameter, alterable composition addition, and changeable appearance for filtering air pollution [14]. Titanium dioxide (TiO2) is the most common chemical used in photocatalysis reaction because of its simple synthesis process, low cost, and high efficiency in the elimination of air pollution; however, it is difficult to separate powder TiO2 from solution as a defect of TiO2 [15]. One solution of using powder TiO2 as a photocatalysis application is by attaching powder TiO2 to a carrier (fiber membrane or other application of Li-ion battery [16–18]).

The innovative material of zero-dimension carbon quantum dots possess specific and excellent optical and electronic traits, such as light harvest, adjustable photoluminescence, and electron capture, especially great photoluminescence ability; the energy of light transforms from low-energy to high-energy after multi photon passed through carbon quantum dots [19]. Based on the above backgrounds, in this study, carbon quantum dots/titanium dioxide (QD/TiO2) were employed to conduct photocatalysis reaction. The addition of QD improved the light absorption of TiO2; hence, the above method could efficiently use sunlight to improve photocatalysis ability. Then, polyimide (PI)/polyether sulfone (PES) with QD/TiO2 solution was formed as a nano-fiber membrane through electrospinning technology, and the nano-fiber membrane could efficiently absorb and degrade VOCs. The nano-fiber membrane also acquired traits of nano-diameter, low porosity, low pressure loss and high filtration. In addition to the Taguchi method for factors order analysis, the factors included the content of PI/PES, the content of QD/TiO2, operational voltages, and electrospinning time, which were adjusted to present the optimal photocatalysis efficiency and filtration ability of acetone as VOCs sample.

Experimental samples used in this study contained titanium tetrabutanolate (TBOT) (CAS No.: 5593704), acetic acid (CAS No.: 64197), citric acid (CAS No.: 77929), sodium hydroxide (CAS No.: 1310732), ethanol (CAS No.: 64175), PI (CAS No.: 62929026), PES (CAS No.: 25667429), dimethylacetamide (DMAc) (CAS No.: 127195), and sodium chloride (NaCl) (CAS No.: 7647145), which were of reagent grade, (Alfa Chemistry, BASF Taiwan Ltd., and Acros Organics). The hydrothermal method was applied to produce carbon quantum dots, while 40.0 g citric acid (carbon precursors) was dissolved in the mixture of 60.0 % water and 40.0 % ethanol with a heating temperature of 180.0 °C for 40.0 h. The synthesis process was conducted in a Teflon-lined hydrothermal synthesis autoclave reactor. Subsequently, the brown gel matter formed was evenly dissolved in aqueous NaOH solution (5.0 M, pH 7). At the end, freeze drying procedure generated QD powder.

Five hundred milliliters of a solution containing of 9.7 wt% acetic acid and 17.0 wt% tetrabutyl titanate were held in a 1000 mL beaker and the hydrolysis reaction via magnetic stirring for 2 days. Then, the aqueous solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined hydrothermal synthesis autoclave reactor for further heating at 200.0 °C for 4 h. Generally, the TiO2 nanoparticles were collected through centrifugation after washing five times by absolute alcohol.

Deionized water (DI) (73.3 wt%) was used to dissolve 9.7 wt% acetic acid and 17.0 wt% TBOT was applied to mix and hydrolyze with the aqueous solution for 3 days. Four ratios (0.0, 3.0, 5.0, and 7.0 wt%) of QD was dissolved in 5 mL of DI water, and then this aqueous solution was mixed with 15.0 mL ethanol solution containing titanium dioxide under magnetic stirring process for 2 h. Subsequently, the mixed solution was placed in a hydrothermal autoclave reactor for isothermal heating at 200.0 °C for 4 h. QD/TiO2 powders were generated after centrifugation of the absolute alcohol solution and air-dried process at 70.0 °C.

Fig. 1 A shows a typical installation of the electrospinning process comprising several operating variables, such as flow rate, applied voltage, spinning time, and distance between the collector plate and the spray nozzle. The polymer solution in nozzle sprays was used to generate a nano-meter sized membrane on a collector plate at a 15.0 cm distance between the collector plate with the 21-gauge needle of a 10.0 mL syringe at 24.5–27.5 °C, relative humidity of 45.0 %–50.0 %, and high DC voltage. Three predetermined amounts (3.0, 5.0, and 7.0 wt%) of QD/TiO2 were evenly dispersed in dimethylacetamide with sonication for 1 h. Three concentrations of 38.0, 40.0, and 42.0 wt% PI/PES polymer solution with PI and PES ratio of (30:70) were dissolved in DMAC and stirred at 50.0 °C for 8 h. Using an FES-COS electrospinning apparatus (Falco Co, Taiwan), the mixed PI/PES polymer and QD/TiO2 solutions were used to obtain the QD/TiO2@PI/PES composite membranes.

The functional groups and the surface morphology of QD/TiO2 was analyzed by Fourier transformation infrared spectrophotometer (FTIR; PerkinElmer, Spectrum 100) and a scanning electron microscope (SEM; TESCAN, 5136 MM) equipped with an energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscope (EDS; X-Act, Oxford). The nano-size and atomic level of the photocatalysts were analyzed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM; HITACHI, HT7700) to analyze.

Fig. 1A shows that the photocatalysis activates at the illumination conditions of light sources (254 nm lamp, Philips) and light emitting diode lamps (LED lamps). Here, three wavelengths (365 nm, 500 nm, and 700 nm) were used in the LED lamps, which were applied to assess the decomposition rate of acetone. An LI-COR LI-1800 (LI-COR) was used to analyze the light intensity and wavelength distribution on the external surface of the inner reactor tube. Thermo TVA 1000 (Kaoten) equipped with FID detector was used to detect for the concentration of acetone, while the temperature of the photoreactor was maintained at 25 °Cfor all experiments.

The merits and demerits of the filter efficiency are commonly estimated through the quality of fiber membrane. Fig. 1B shows the appearance of filter testing system and scanning mobility particle sizer is the main mechanism for particle filtration. Here, a Kr-85 neutralizer was applied to lead the particles to acquire positive and negative ions, and fiber membrane could filter particles by electrostatic attraction. By the ASTM Standards (ASTM F2299) and European Standard (EN 14683) [20], the pressure loss and penetration parameters could appropriately evaluate the filter quality, as shown in equation (1).
(1)
Q f
=
( ‐ lnP )
/ Δ P
Where ΔP, P, and Q
f represented the pressure drop across the filter (Pa), the particle penetration and filter quality (Pa−1), respectively.

In this study, SEM was used to observe the morphology of the sample. The synthesized TiO2 and QD/TiO2 appeared as uniformed sphere particle, as shown in Fig. 2 . As shown in Fig. 2B, coagulation phenomenon occurred in QD/TiO2, and caused variation of its physical traits, including specific surface area, particle size, and optical properties. An ultrasonic bath was used to separate the coagulated TiO2 group for uniform distribution into the electrospinning solution, which could decrease the coagulation phenomenon. Chen et al. [21] found that a coagulation phenomenon occurred during the synthesis of QD/TiO2. According to the study by Mahbubul et al., ultrasonic treatment generated positive effects on the fluid dispersion property of nano-particle, and the most stable property occurred after 150 min of ultrasonic treatment [22].

Fig. 2C shows the TEM image of synthesized QD/TiO2, and the particle size of QD/TiO2 was calculated in the range of 17–20 nm using ImageJ software. Fig. 2D presents the appearance of QD, wherein it acquired a high energy gap even while the particle size was small due to the quantum confinement effect. The synthesized QD solution appeared blue under UV emission because of the blue light shifting effect, as presented in Fig. 2E and F.

FTIR was used to analyze the red infrared absorption of TiO2 and 5.0 % QD/TiO2; a wide absorption peak was observed at 3200 cm−1, which represented as specific functional group of OH (Fig. 3 A). The C=O group showed peaks at 1157 cm−1 and 1750 cm−1, and the C–O– C group peaked at 1380 cm−1 from the analysis of 5% QD/TiO2. Furthermore, the Ti–O–Ti group peaked at 800 cm−1 for TiO2 and 5% QD/TiO2 in this study. The reason for the above phenomenon may be due to the addition of QD. Hu et al. [19] found that the synthesized QD depicted the group of OH at 1635 cm−1 and 3420 cm−1 and the peak at 2974 cm−1 represented the CH2 group. Moreover, the C–H group peaked at 1226 cm−1, while the peak at 1157 cm−1 represented the C=O group [19]. The synthesized QD/TiO2 was successful because the result of our study was similar to that of the study by Hu [19].

The relationship between material structure and its trait are closely interconnected, and functional group analysis can reveal the structure of material through FTIR analysis. The peak of PI appeared at 3064 cm−1 as a specific aromatic group, the peak of C–H showed at 2958 and 2865 cm− 1 as a fatty group, the peak of C=O appeared at 1776 and 1720 cm−1 as the carbonyl functional group, the peak of C–N appeared at 1375 cm−1, the peak of C–N–C appeared at 1295 cm−1, the peak of C–O–C was at 1243 cm−1, and the peak of C–H was at the position of 720 cm−1 [23–30]. Moreover, the peak of PES appeared at 1577 cm−1 as a specific peak of the aromatic benzene ring group, the peak of C–O showed at 1482 and 1105 cm−1, the peak of C–O–C displayed at 1242 cm−1, and the peak of C–H presented at 1150 cm−1 [31–35]. Fig. 3B presents similar results as mentioned in above studies, and the PI/PES spectrum proves that PI and PES still preserve its original trait after generating PI/PES membrane via electrospinning technology. Furthermore, results in Fig. 3B also confirm that PI/PES is a polymer blend and no chemical reaction occurred in the synthesis of PI/PES. Although the specific peak of TiO2 did not appear in the 5% QD/TiO2@PI/PES spectrum, we assumed the addition of a small amount of TiO2 caused no vivid peak presented in the QD/TiO2@PI/PES spectrum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalyst tests using methylene blue and acetone
ce:section.ce:para: Besides the photocatalytic effect on liquid pollutant (MB solution), the QD/TiO2 membrane also could degrade gas pollutant (acetone), while the concentration of acetone was 150 ppm for degradation test. Under optimal condition (Taguchi 5: 40.0% PI/PES, 5.0% QD/TiO2, 25.0 kV, 2 h), the membrane showed better photocatalytic efficiency than the P25 membrane, as shown in Fig. 4. Approximately 87.0% of acetone was degraded after 5 h photocatalytic process via the membrane with the optimal condition (Taguchi 5). Furthermore, TiO2 and QD required photocatalysis reaction, so XPS analysis was used to present photoelectron intensity of TiO2 and QD. Appendixes Figure A showed the XPS analysis diagram of TiO2 and QD/TiO2.

The antibacterial process involves photocatalysis reaction and ionic antibacterial mechanism for analysis of nano-metal oxides. This study used QD/TiO2 to conduct antibacterial test on Staphylococcus aureus, where UV with wavelength of 365 nm emitted on the solution contained of QD/TiO2 and S. aureus with a concentration of 105 CFU/g, and a sampling per hour with duration of 24 h was adopted to observe the outcome of antibacterial test, as shown in Fig. 6 . The concentration of S. aureus did not vary obviously after 1 h of UV emission, and then decreased slightly after 2 h. Most S. aureus cells were removed after 3 h of UV emission, and almost all S. aureus were killed after 4 h. As a result, QD loaded on TiO2 could improve the absorption efficiency of TiO2 to accelerate the separation of photoinduced carriers, while the humidity and oxygen of bacterial solution reacted with the electron-hole of TiO2 to form free radical of OH− group and O2 − ion, thus, S. aureus could not survive due to the destruction of bacterial cell membrane via reaction of OH− group and O2 − ion. Yan et al. [37], proposed the antimicrobial property of QD/TiO2 and its activity against Escherichia coli and S. aureus, while the antibacterial efficiency of E. coli and S. aureus approached 90.9 % and 92.8 % with 1 μg/mL QD/TiO2 for 24 h under visible light irradiation. QD/TiO2 significantly improved the absorption of visible light to generate more free radicals under visible light irradiation than the sample without QD/TiO2 addition. Organic matter within the cells were destroyed and eventually caused the degradation of bacteria due to the attack of free radicals. The OH− group, h+ group, and O2 − ion were detected by free radical capture experiment. Therefore, the photocatalysis of QD/TiO2 were confirmed to present antibacterial efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of filtration quality of nano-fiber membrane via Taguchi method (The larger the better)
ce:section.ce:para: This study used the Taguchi method to analyze the filtration quality of QD/TiO2@PI/PES nano-fiber membrane, and four factors, including concentrations of PI/PES and QD/TiO2, operational voltage, and electrospinning time were used to obtain the optimal filtration quality. Each factor was examined at three levels to observe the variations in filtration quality at different combinations of factors. The contents included PI/PES (38.0 wt%, 40.0 wt%, and 42.0 wt%), QD/TiO2 (3.0 wt%, 5.0 wt%, and 7.0 wt%), the operational voltage (20.0 kV, 22.5 kV, and 25.0 kV) and the electrospinning time of 2 h, 4 h, and 6 h. The signal-to-noise (S/N) value presented strengths and weaknesses on the stability of factors during the analysis of various combinations of factors; a high S/N value represented the great factor quality. Therefore, ‘the larger the better’ condition was suitable to analyze filtration quality, and the equation for the condition of ‘the larger the better’ is depicted in equation (2). Table 1 presents various S/N values at different combinations of factors as the indicator of membrane's filtration quality. The factors were designed as three classes based on the contents of PI/PES (38.0 wt%, 40.0 wt%, and 42.0 wt%), while other three factor were arranged randomly. As the result, there were nine S/N values from the combinations of factors with the calculation of L9 (34).

The condition of ‘the larger the better’ was applied to those four factors with three classes; thus, each factor acquired three S/N values as depicted in Table 2 . The electrospinning time exhibited the largest S/N value (−18.54), and the subsequent S/N values (−21.79, −22.05, and −22.52, respectively) were attributed by contents of PI/PES, QD/TiO2, and operational voltage. Besides, the S/N values changed significantly in relation to the factor of electrospinning time with the value of 5.53, followed by variation in S/N values (3.75, 1.31, and 1.25, respectively) in relation to contents of PI/PES, QD/TiO2, and operational voltage. Therefore, the order of influencing factors was electrospinning time, contents of PI/PES, content of QD/TiO2, and operational voltage.

Based on results of the Taguchi method, tests were conducted following the conditions of Taguchi method to verify the real filtration efficiency and quality. Table 3 presents all filtration efficiencies valued above 95.0 %. The filtration efficiency exhibited a declining trend with increase in electrospinning time. Increasing the electrospinning time results in the formation of a firm nano-fiber membrane, inducing a reduction in pore diameter and increase in pressure resistance; such a firm nano-fiber membrane may experience a large pressure loss during filtration process, even if it was possible to filter more number of particles compared to that by thin nano-fiber membrane [38,39]. Accordingly, filtration efficiency was determined by the thickness of the fiber membrane.

Considering ‘Larger-the-better’ condition:
(2)
S / N = − 10 × log
(
1 n
∑
i = 1
n
1
y i 2
)

ce:section.ce:section-title: Variation in filtration quality at different electrospinning time
ce:section.ce:para: Under the condition of 2 h of electrospinning time, the highest filtration quality was observed involving Taguchi 1 (38.0 wt% PI/PES, 3.0 wt% QD/TiO2, 20.0 kV, 2 h), Taguchi 5 (40.0 wt% PI/PES, 5.0 wt% QD/TiO2, 25.0 kV, 2 h), and Taguchi 9 (42.0 wt% PI/PES, 7.0 wt% QD/TiO2, 22.5 kV, 2 h). The secondary filtration quality was observed at the electrospinning time of 4 h involving Taguchi 2 (38.0 wt% PI/PES, 5.0 wt% QD/TiO2, 22.5 kV, 4 h), Taguchi 6 (40.0 wt% PI/PES, 7.0 wt% QD/TiO2, 20.0 kV, 4 h), and Taguchi 7 (42.0 wt% PI/PES, 7.0 wt% QD/TiO2, 22.5 kV, 4 h) The lowest filtration quality was observed when the electrospinning time was 6 h, involving Taguchi 3 (38.0 wt% PI/PES, 7.0 wt% QD/TiO2, 25.0 kV, 6 h), Taguchi 4 (40.0 wt% PI/PES, 3.0 wt% QD/TiO2, 22.5 kV, 6 h), and Taguchi 8 (42.0 wt% PI/PES, 5.0 wt% QD/TiO2, 20.0 kV, 6 h). With slight adjustment of the optimal conditions of Taguchi 5, the best filtration efficiency and quality were 99.6 % and 0.151, respectively, at 40.0 wt% PI/PES, 5.0 wt% QD/TiO2, 20.0 kV, and 2 h, as shown in Table 3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Variation of filtration quality at different ratios of PI/PES
ce:section.ce:para: According to Table 3, the condition of best filter efficiency and quality included 40.0 wt% PI/PES, 5.0 wt% QD/TiO2, 20.0 kV, and 2 h. Based on the best condition, different ratios of PI/PES (10:90, 20:80, 30:70, and 40:60) were employed to test the variations in filtration efficiency and quality. Fig. 7 A-D shows the morphology of PI/PES fiber membrane at different ratios; the thickness of the fiber increased significantly when the ratio of PI increased above 30.0 %. Then, filter efficiency and quality were examined to assess the effect of different ratios of PI/PES (Fig. 8A and 9 A). The filter efficiency increased with decrease in PES ratio, because a high ratio of PES increased the viscosity of the electrospinning solution, resulting in increased fiber diameter. Galikhanov et al. [40] proposed that stacks of thin fiber diameter result in intense porosity which effectively block numerous particles; conversely, stacks of thicker fiber diameter result in loose porosity, leading to less particles being blocked. Our study presented similar result to Galikhanov et al. [40], and stacks of thin fiber diameter resulted in membrane with intense porosity and a high filter efficiency.

Table 4 presents the trend of pressure loss in the fiber membrane, which decreased as the PES ratio decreased, as a result of the effect of membrane thickness. The stack of the membrane could arrange intensely when the fiber diameter was very thin; as a result, the thickness of membrane was reduced and caused only a small pressure loss. When the ratio of PI/PES was 10:90, the fiber diameter was thick to generate loose stack of fiber membrane, leading to the largest pressure loss. The fiber diameter decreased when the ratio of PI/PES was adjusted to 20:80, which rendered the stack of fiber membrane to be more intense, resulting in a low pressure loss. When the ratio of PI/PES was changed to 30:70, the fiber diameter decreased, resulting in the smallest value of pressure loss.

The droplet phenomena occurred when the PI/PES ratio was 40:60 because of the influence of solution viscosity, and the filtration efficiency of the fiber membrane containing droplet was affected. Moreover, it was difficult to stabilize the electrospinning solution at the PI/PES ratio of 40:60, so, the air resistance increased accordingly, accompanied with more pressure loss. Therefore, the optimal ratio of PI/PES was 30:70, when the filtration efficiency approached to more than 98.0% with the lowest pressure loss, and the best filtration quality of 0.131. Gonçalves et al. [40], used polyacrylonitrile (PAN) to produce fiber membrane. They found that the fiber membrane with heavy thickness could block numerous particles, accompanied by the problem of high air resistance due to the increase in pressure loss from both sides of fiber membrane.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Variation in filtration quality at different operational voltages
ce:section.ce:para: During the electrospinning process, the operational voltage should exceed the minimum critical voltage to form a fiber membrane with the shape of a Taylor cone [41,42]. Results in Table 2 show that the order of factors influencing the operational voltages ranged was the lowest, suggesting that the variation in operational voltages might not obviously affect filtration quality. However, we also conducted different tests to assess filtration quality at different operational voltages, keeping the reference condition as 40.0 wt% PI/PES, 5.0 wt% QD/TiO2, 20.0 kV, and, 2 h; here and four levels (17.5 kV, 20.0 kV, 22.5 kV, and 25.0 kV) were examined to observe the changes in surface and its filtration quality of the fiber membrane.

Table 5 shows the trend of filtration efficiency and quality from upstream to downstream, when the operational voltage varied from 17.5 kV to 25.0 kV. Except the filtration efficiency at 17.5 kV, other filtration efficiencies at 20.0 kV, 22.5 kV, and 25.0 kV, were above 99.5%. Moreover, Fig. 7E-H depicts the SEM test of the morphology of fiber membrane at various operational voltages. At 17.5 kV, the strength of operational voltage could not force the electrospinning solution to form fiber membrane with great Taylor cone shape; this was accompanied with much lower filtration efficiency, compared with other filtration efficiencies. When operational voltage was increased to 22.5 kV and 25.0 kV, the induced strength of operational voltage exceeded the stretching limit of the electrospinning solution and caused uneven distribution condition of fiber membrane with droplet generation on the surface of the membrane, accompanied with increase in pressure loss of the fiber membrane. Filtration efficiency and quality presented the best value at 20.0 kV (Fig. 8B and 9B), because the increase in operational voltage could increase electric density and further improve charge repulsion and stretching strength of electrospinning solution, thus, resulting in the formation of nano-fiber membrane with small diameter [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Variation of filtration quality at different contents of QD/TiO2
ce:section.ce:para: The filtration efficiency of fiber membrane generally increased with thickness, but also further influenced pressure loss of fiber membrane, resulting in a decline in filtration quality. Corona discharge is commonly applied to increase the surface potential of fiber membrane to store charge on the fiber membrane, and the electrostatic attraction can attract or repel particles, thereby improving filtration efficiency [44]. Furthermore, the addition of nano metal particle, such as TiO2, could enhance the potential of the membrane through the interaction of electric charge on the fiber membrane, thus enhancing its surface potential, which could affect the strength of electrostatic attraction during filtration process of micro and nano particles [45–47].

Different contents of QD/TiO2 were applied to examine the variation in filtration quality. The reference condition was 40.0 wt% PI/PES, 5.0 wt% QD/TiO2, 20.0 kV, and the concentration of QD/TiO2 varied as 1.0 wt%, 3.0 wt%, 5.0 wt %, and 7.0 wt %. Different contents of QD/TiO2 caused variations in the surface potential of the fiber membrane. Consequently, the filtration mechanism changed from the initial conditions of interception, inertial impaction, and diffusion to a mechanism driven by electrostatic attraction. Therefore, the sodium chloride particle, which carries positive charges was easily captured on the fiber membrane due to electrostatic attraction, and this effect was more obvious when the particle diameter was small, as shown in Fig. 7I–L. Feng et al., showed that electrostatic attraction could attract or repel many particles when the particle size was small [48,49]. In this study, particle with size of 10–100 nm was used as sample for filtration efficiency test. When the addition of QD/TiO2 increased from 1 wt% to 5.0 %, the surface potential of fiber membrane acquired the smallest value. As the result, fiber membrane could not block the small sized sodium chloride particle. Furthermore, the addition of QD/TiO2 improved the filtration efficiency and quality, because the increased concentration of QD/TiO2 could enhance the surface potential of the fiber membrane, and small size particle could attach on the fiber membrane due to electrostatic attraction.

Fig. 8C and 9C show that surface potential of the fiber membrane declined when addition of QD/TiO2 was increased to 7.0 wt%, because QD/TiO2 caused polarization phenomenon when high-voltage electric field was applied in the electrospinning process. Moreover, the inner electric field of QD/TiO2 exhibited polarized segregation on positive and negative electric charge leading to variation in electric potential. When the content of QD/TiO2 was increased, the assembling phenomenon occurred due to the interaction of electric charges to neutralize positive and negative electric charge, resulting in the decrease in surface potential of the fiber membrane to influence its filtration efficiency.

A Comparison of filtration results using different filter materials are shown in Table 6 [50–54]. All filter materials achieved more than 95.0% filtration efficiency, accompanied with good filtration quality for the removal of nanometer particles. Based on the standards ASTM F2299 [20] and the reports [54], CQD/TiO2@PI/PES membranes can be suitably utilized as a potential material for preparing level 3 medical face masks (Filtration efficiency>98.0% and ΔP＜58.8 Pa/cm2) and N95 masks (Filtration efficiency>95.0% and ΔP＜99.64 Pa). Bortolassi et al., investigated silver/polyacrylonitrile (Ag/PAN) electrospun fibers acquired approaching 100% and 0.05 Pa-1 of filtration efficiency and filtration quality for removal of aerosol particles with 9–300 nm diameters, accompanying with removal of above 104 CFU/mL E. coli bacteria [55,56]. Furthermore, QD/TiO2@PI/PES membrane exhibited the lowest pressure loss compared with other filter materials, and could degrade solid/liquid and gas pollutants. Therefore, QD/TiO2@PI/PES membrane can not only could block nanometer particles but also degrade liquid and gas pollutants by photocatalysis reaction.

In this study, electrospinning technology was used to successfully synthesize QD/TiO2@PI/PES nano-fiber membrane, and by SEM and TEM tests showed that the morphology of fiber membrane exhibited even spherical distribution with particle size of 17–20 nm. The MB degradation test depicted great result (about 45.5%) for the synthesized QD/TiO2@PI/PES membrane than the commercial TiO2 membrane (P25) (less 10.0%). The nano-fiber membrane acquired excellent antibacterial function against E. coli, while 365 nm UV light continually emitted on E. coli for 4 h, and 100.0 % antibacterial efficiency could be achieved when 5.0% QD/TiO2 solution was mixed with E. coli under the above conditions. According to Taguchi tests, the best operational parameters of filter efficiency and quality was 99.6 % and 0.151, respectively, when the QD/TiO2@PI/PES nano-fiber membrane was formed at 40.0 wt% PI/PES, 5.0 wt% QD/TiO2, 20.0 kV, 2 h. When the content of PI/PES was too low, the droplet foamed on the surface of fiber membrane, resulting in less contact times on QD/TiO2 and VOCs to induce reduction of photocatalyst efficacy of the fiber membrane. Furthermore, due to increase in the pressure loss of the fiber membrane, the air flow rate accordingly decreased, and the filtration efficiency also declined.

With variation in QD/TiO2 content, the photocatalyst efficacy of the fiber membrane enhanced with increase in QD/TiO2 addition, and the most efficient photocatalyst activity occurred at when 5.0 wt% QD/TiO2 was used. However, the photocatalyst efficacy of the fiber membrane declined when the content of QD/TiO2 was adjusted to 7.0 wt%, because high photocatalyst concentration caused accumulation of particles to induce poor light penetration efficiency and increase of light scattering. The addition of QD/TiO2 improved the surface potential of the fiber membrane to cause fiber membrane to acquire a positive electric charge, thus, the particle could be filtered due to electrostatic attraction. When the addition of QD/TiO2 was increased, the inner QD/TiO2 occurred interaction on positive and negative electric charges, and the surface potential declined subsequently. As the result, addition of 5.0 wt% of QD/TiO2 acquired the highest surface potential of 1.60 ± 0.25 to result in a filtration efficiency and quality of 99.9 % and 0.1337, respectively, with consequent acetone degradation of 87.1%. The voltage was too low to produce a homogeneous fiber jet. When the voltage was too high, it destabilized the solution jet and led to a sharp bending of the fibers with droplet generation, thus increasing the pressure loss to obtain the best filtration quality at 20.0 kV. With the increase in electrospinning time, the photocatalytic ability could be enhanced; this was attributed to the increase in QD/TiO2 loading onto the nano-fiber membrane, although in terms of filtration, this increased the pressure loss of the nano-fiber membrane. Nonetheless, it still acquired good filtration quality and filtration efficiency for the removal of particle and VOCs.

Yu-Lin Kuo: supports, Conceptualization, for this MS. Wei-Ting Chen: supports, Writing – original draft, for this MS. Chien-Hua Chen: supports, Methodology, for this MS. Hung-Ta Wu: supports, Methodology, for this MS. Chyow-San Chiou: supports, Investigation, for this MS. Ying-Cih Chen: supports, Data curation, and, Formal analysis, for this MS. Hua-Wei Chen: supports, Writing – review & editing, for this MS.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests:

Hua-Wei Chen reports writing assistance was provided by National Ilan University. Yu-Lin Kuo reports writing assistance was provided by National Taiwan University of Science and Technology. Wei-Ting Chen reports writing assistance was provided by St Mary's Junior College of Medicine Nursing and Management. Chien-Hua Chen, Hung-Ta Wu, Chyow-San Chiou, Ying-Cih Chen report writing assistance was provided by National Ilan University. Hua-Wei Chen reports a relationship with National Ilan University that includes: employment.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported in part by grant NSTC 112-2221-E-197-009 from the National Science and Technology Council, Republic of China.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XPS analysis diagram of TiO2 and QD/TiO2.
Morphology of synthesized QD/TiO2@PI/PES by SEM (high multiple resolution). *The multiple of (A) and (B) are 50,000.

Rashkov, “Study of charge storage in the nanofibrous poly(ethylene terephthalate) electrets prepared by electrospinning or by corona discharge method,” European Polymer Journal, vol. 44, no. 7, pp. 1962-1967, 2008/07/01/, 2008.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Improvement of the electret properties of polystyrene with introduction of titanium dioxide

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. F. Galikhanov, “Improvement of the Electret Properties of Polystyrene with Introduction of Titanium Dioxide,” Surface Engineering and Applied Electrochemistry, vol. 54, no. 1, pp. 111-116, 2018/01/01, 2018.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Further improvement of air filtration efficiency of cellulose filters coated with nanofibers via inclusion of electrostatically active nanoparticles

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Cho, A. Naydich, M. W. Frey, and Y. L. Joo, “Further improvement of air filtration efficiency of cellulose filters coated with nanofibers via inclusion of electrostatically active nanoparticles,” Polymer, vol. 54, no. 9, pp. 2364-2372, 2013/04/19/, 2013.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Experimental and theoretical study of a novel electrostatic enhanced air filter (EEAF) for fine particles

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Feng, Z. Long, and J. Mo, “Experimental and theoretical study of a novel electrostatic enhanced air filter (EEAF) for fine particles,” Journal of Aerosol Science, vol. 102, pp. 41-54, 2016/12/01/, 2016.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A newly developed electrostatic enhanced pleated air filters towards the improvement of energy and filtration efficiency

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Feng, and S.-J. Cao, “A newly developed electrostatic enhanced pleated air filters towards the improvement of energy and filtration efficiency,” Sustainable Cities and Society, vol. 49, pp. 101569, 2019/08/01/, 2019.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Super-hydrophobic cellulose nanofiber air filter with highly efficient filtration and humidity resistance

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Liu T, Cai C, Ma R, Deng Y, Tu L, Fan Y, Lu D, Super-hydrophobic Cellulose Nanofiber Air Filter with Highly Efficient Filtration and Humidity Resistance. ACS Appl Mater Interfaces 2021;13(20):24032-24041. https://DOI:10.1021/acsami.1c04258.
